english: Lt., dre-mo (dre mo) Mng., *ḍe-móṅ* W., elbow.

1. any dirt that is removed by scraping, whereas dri-ma (dri ma) is washed off; more particularly: -- 2. soot, which is also used as a medicine Wdn.; k'uṅ-drég (khung dreg) id.; sgróṅ-dreg (sgrong dreg) lamp-black; slaṅ-dreg (slang dreg) soot on a kettle; lcágs-dreg (lcags dreg) v. lcags (lcags); t'ál-dreg (thal dreg), rdó-dreg (rdo dreg) Med.? -- só-dreg (so dreg) tartar incrusting the teeth Med. -- dreg-bál (dreg bal) flakes of soot. -- dreg-nád (dreg nad) gout; dreg-grúm (dreg grum) id.

1. pride, haughtiness, arrogance, k'eṅ-ḍégs (kheng dregs) id.; drégs-pa nyams (dregs pa nyams) pride is put down, humbled; drégs-pa skyaṅ-ba (dregs pa skyang ba) to lay aside, to put off pride; nór-gyis dregs (nor gyis dregs) purse-proud Lex. -- 2. proud, haughty, arrogant, = drégs-pa-can (dregs pa can); drégs-pa (-can) t'ams-cád (dregs pa can thams cad) the great, the proud, the people of high rank, the great ones of this world Pth.; in the world of spirits, with or without bgegs (bgegs): the powerful demons. -- 3. as a vb.: ró-tsas dregs t'se (ro rtsas dregs tshe) when the sexual impulse is strong Med.

(Zam. तरक्षु) hyena, which name has prob. been transferred by the inhabitants of the mountainous districts to the dred (dred), an animal better known to them) the yellow bear; mi-dred (mi dred) a bear that devours men Mil.; p'yúgs-dred (phyugs dred) a bear destructive to cattle; dréd-po (dred po) he-bear, dréd-mo (dred mo) she-bear. -- dred-ts'áṅ (dred tshang) a bear's den. -- dred-siu-śiṅ (dred siu shing) hazel-nut tree Sch.

1. Sch.: 'evasive, lazy', yet č'os-méd dréd-po zol-zóg g.yo-rgyú-can (chos med dred po zol zog g.yo rgyu can)? -- 2. load, burden, esp. a heavy load C., dréd-po dréd-pa (dred po dred pa) = krés-po grág-pa (kres po grag pa), to cord a load.
rtsa-dréd-ma (rtsa dred ma) Glr. = drés-ma (dres ma); dám-dréd-ma (dam dred ma) Mil.?
v. dre (dre); dreu-rṅóg (dre'u rngog) 1. the mane of a mule. -- 2. a couch, or stuffed-seat Cs. -- 3. a kind of long-haired cloth.

1. C. a kind of grass, of which ropes and shoes (of great durability) are made; Glr. dréd-ma (dred ma); drés-mai ge-sár (dres mai ge sar) S.g. the filaments of drés-ma (dres ma); dres-bru (dres bru) Cs., dres-brum (dres brum) S.g. the seeds of drés-ma (dres ma). -- 2. W. Iris kamaonensis.

(cf. dró-ba (dro ba)), 1. the hot time of the day, from about 9 o'cl. a. m. till 3 o'cl. p. m.; dró-la báb-nas (dro la bab nas) when this time arrived Dzl.; sṅá-dro (snga dro) the morning, p'yí-dro (phyi dro) 1. the later part of the afternoon, 2. W. *pi-ro* evening, night. -- 2. a meal taken about noon, lunch; dro btáb-pa (dro btab pa) to lunch; dro-lúg (dro lug) a sheep intended to be eaten for a luncheon; dro-śá (dro sha) meat intended for such a purpose.
1. to be warm, v. drós-pa (dros pa); gen. adj. warm, dró-bai gnas (dro bai gnas) a warm place; dró-bar gyúr-ba (dro bar gyur ba) to grow warm. -- 2. warmth (bág-dro (bag dro) v. sub ur (ur)).
Sch.: 'packed up, made up into pack or parcel'.
a large basket or dosser, provided with a lid, and carried on the back, Hind. पिटारा.

1. warmth, heat, e.g. of the sun; drod-gśér (drod gsher) warmth and moisture; dród-kyi śiṅ (drod kyi shing) a tropical tree Wdn.; me-drod (me drod) 1. the heat of the fire Lt. 2. prob. animal heat, perh. because it is supposed to arise from a union of the fiery element with a germ originated by conception. -- 2. k'a-dród (kha drod) zuṅ gćig (zung gcig) a small piece of food, = k'a-zás (kha zas), and prob. incorrect for k'a-bród (kha brod) enjoyment of the mouth. -- lám-la drod t'ób-pa (lam la drod thob pa) Mil. was explained: to have a cheerful mind, free from doubts and apprehensions on the way (to heaven), drod (drod), therefore, seems to stand here for brod (brod). -- drod-rtags (drod rtags), Mil., was explained as being new knowledge, new perceptions, as a fruit of long meditation; one Lex. has dród-rig-pa (drod rig pa) = मात्रज्ञ experienced or well-versed in measure.
col. warm, zan-drón (zan dron) warm food.
Sch. = dro (dro); dros-č'én (dros chen) noon, mid-day, dros-č'uṅ (dros chung) forenoon (?).
pf. of dró-ba (dro ba), heated, grown warm, esp. of the ground by the
